text.title: Outsource PR Etiquette Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the outsource-pr-etiquette methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: A PR carries exactly one intent (one feature, one fix, or one refactor). Mixed-intent PRs are split before opening.
rule.rationale: Multi-intent PRs force the reviewer to context-switch mid-review and dilute the audit trail.

rule.statement: Diff size is ≤400 changed lines excluding lockfile updates and generated code. PRs over the cap are split or flagged with an explicit waiver.
rule.rationale: Reviewer accuracy collapses past ~400 LOC; SmartBear's empirical data shows defect detection drops sharply after 300-500 lines.

rule.statement: Every PR names a single primary reviewer (handle, not team). Teams may be added as secondary.
rule.rationale: Named ownership eliminates the bystander effect; teams diffuse responsibility.

rule.statement: Body contains a Repro section with the exact commands (clone, checkout branch, run) and the expected output that proves the change works.
rule.rationale: Outsource reviewers cannot ping back for missing steps; the PR must be self-contained.

rule.statement: Behaviour changes ship with before/after screenshots, log snippets, or response payloads attached in the PR body.
rule.rationale: Words describe what changed; evidence proves it. Reviewers anchor on the artefact, not the prose.

rule.statement: Each PR states the rollback in one sentence (revert SHA, feature-flag toggle, db rollback steps).
rule.rationale: If we cannot back out the change in one step, the PR is too large or too coupled.

rule.statement: If reviewer is co-located AND synchronous review is the team norm, OR PR is a trivial typo/config bump, skip this methodology.
rule.rationale: Forcing the outsource shape on co-located synchronous review wastes prep time without lowering latency.
